mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz A-Class A160 / A180 petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Mercedes-Benz A-Class A160/A180 petrol engine costs £1,500–£4,000 supply only, varying by generation: M166 £1,200–£2,500, M266 £1,500–£3,000, M270 £2,500–£4,500, M282 £3,000–£5,500. Fitting adds £900–£1,500. Check the Era Map above for your engine code.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz A-Class A160 / A180 petrol reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: That depends on which generation. The W168 M166 has a sealed timing chain that can fail. The W169 M266 is one of Mercedes' more reliable compact engines. The W176 M270 is generally reliable but timing chain stretch and carbon build-up are known issues. The W177 M282 is modern and generally reliable. Buy the W169 or W177 for the best reliability.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz A-Class A160 / A180 petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: W168 M166: timing chain failure (cold-start rattle, £1,000–£2,500). W176 M270: timing chain stretch (cold-start rattle, £1,500–£3,000), ignition faults (misfires, starting issues, £200–£600), carbon build-up (walnut blasting, £400–£800), thermostat housing leaks (£300–£600). W177 M282: carbon build-up, generally fewer issues.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz A-Class A160 / A180 petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: A-Class values range from £1,000–£25,000 depending on generation, condition and mileage. A £1,500–£4,000 reconditioned engine represents 15–80% of vehicle value — our 70% Mercedes-Benz scrap threshold means careful evaluation is required. For a well-maintained W176 or W177, replacement is often justified. For an ageing W168 with other issues, assess scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz A-Class A160 / A180 petrol engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The A160/A180 has used four engine families: M166 (W168, 1997–2004), M266 (W169, 2004–2012), M270 DE16 LA (W176, 2012–2018), and M282 (W177, 2018–present). Check your VIN or registration year. The code is stamped on the engine block.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M270 timing chain issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. Timing chain and guide replacement costs £1,500–£3,000 and is significantly cheaper than a full engine replacement (£2,500–£4,500). Replacing the chain, guides and tensioner is recommended around 80,000–100,000 miles. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately — ignored chain wear can lead to catastrophic engine failure.

eraMap.title: Mercedes-Benz A-Class A160 / A180 Petrol — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: Early sandwich-floor A-Class — timing chain is sealed and can snap
Evolved M266 — more reliable, but injectors and throttle body need care
1.6L turbo — timing chain wear and carbon build-up are key watchpoints
Modern 1.3L turbo with cylinder shutoff — generally reliable

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz A-Class A160 / A180 Petrol Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market ranges across all four A-Class petrol generations. Older W168/W169 engines typically run lower on the used market. Reconditioned units for W176/W177 are higher due to turbo and direct injection complexity. See the Era Map above for generation-specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an A-Class petrol engine typically takes 8–12 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The M270 turbo and M282 add complexity.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz A-Class A160 / A180 Petrol — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — if caught before chain snaps

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes-Benz A-Class A160 / A180 Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * W169 (2004–2012) M266 — one of the more reliable A-Class petrol engines
* W176 (2012–2018) with documented timing chain service history
* W177 (2018+) with full service history — the M282 is generally reliable
* Full service history with regular oil changes and MB-approved oil
* W168 with evidence of timing chain inspection — early chains can fail
avoidIf: * W168 with any cold-start rattle — timing chain failure is serious
* W176 with no timing chain history — chain stretch is a known issue
* Any A-Class with poor service history — these engines are unforgiving of neglect
* M270 with misfire or rough idle without documented diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The A160/A180 petrol badge spans Mercedes' entire compact history — from the innovative but flawed W168 to the modern W177. The W169 M266 is arguably the sweet spot for reliability, while the W176 M270 offers more power but demands timing chain vigilance. The W177 M282 is generally reliable with modern tech. Buy the newest you can afford, maintain the timing chain and ignition system, and the A-Class will serve you well.

cta.label: → Compare Mercedes-Benz A-Class A160/A180 petrol engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz A-Class A160 / A180 Petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle on startup — W168 and M270 chain wear warning
* Service History — Check the year to identify which engine — see the Era Map above
* Timing Chain History — W168 and M270: chains inspected/replaced? Critical
* Ignition System — Any misfires, rough idle, or starting issues? (M270 common)
* Carbon Build-up — M270/M282: has intake had a walnut blast? (every 60k miles recommended)
* Cooling System — Any temperature fluctuations? (M270 thermostat housing leaks)
* Oil Leaks — M270: check valve cover gaskets for leaks
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Resolve before buying
